text.title: Negotiation and Persuasion Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Author claims 'I'll walk away' as BATNA; in conversation panics when counterparty calls bluff.
antipattern.root-cause: BATNA was a feeling, not an option.
antipattern.fix: Compute concrete BATNA: who/what/value; reject prep brief without it.

antipattern.symptom: Brief lists 'we want 120K' on both sides; no path to creative bundling.
antipattern.root-cause: Author thought in positions, not interests.
antipattern.fix: Force interests-mapping step before drafting terms.

antipattern.symptom: Author claims 'we have 2 other buyers' when none exist; counterparty discovers it; trust destroyed.
antipattern.fix: Tag only true scarcity; if scarcity is dubious, drop the lever.

antipattern.symptom: Counterparty anchors first at 60K; author counters from 100K; final lands closer to 60K than to author's target.

root-cause.source: Fisher & Ury — Getting to Yes; Galinsky anchoring studies

antipattern.fix: Anchor first when you have credible objective criteria; if not, defer to mutual benchmark.
